activity_details.activity_name: Palmerston Youth Community Garden & Sustainability Skills Program
activity_details.location_of_activity: Palmerston Community Garden, Lot 425 University Avenue, Palmerston NT 0830

community_benefit__alignment.funding_benefit_to_palmerston_community:
The Palmerston Youth Community Garden & Sustainability Skills Program is designed to directly benefit local young people (primarily ages 12–25) and, through them, the broader Palmerston community.

Our association has been running informal weekend meet-ups for migrant and refugee-background youth at the Palmerston Community Garden for the past 18 months. We have consistently had 15–25 young people attend from suburbs including Driver, Gray, Woodroffe, and Moulden. Many of these young people have told us they feel disconnected from school and formal sports, but they are eager to do something practical and hands-on where they can also contribute to the community.

The proposed program will:

1. Provide a safe, structured, alcohol- and drug-free space for young people
   - We will run weekly Saturday morning sessions (9:00am–12:00pm) for 12 weeks during the dry season, supervised by at least two adults (including myself and another committee member with an Ochre Card). This gives young people a regular, predictable place to be that is positive and supervised, which several parents have requested in conversations with us at previous events.

2. Build practical life and employability skills
   - Participants will learn basic horticulture (soil preparation, composting, propagation, watering schedules, pest control), use of simple tools (hand trowels, rakes, wheelbarrows, watering systems), and basic project planning (designing garden beds, noting planting and harvest dates). One of our volunteers, Daniel Nguyen, is a qualified horticulturalist who has offered to run three of the sessions specifically on soil health, seasonal planting in the Top End, and low-water gardening techniques.
   - These skills are directly transferable to entry-level work in landscaping, parks and gardens, nurseries, and hospitality (e.g., understanding fresh produce and herbs). Several of our older participants (17–19) have already asked for help putting these skills on their resumes.

3. Promote healthy eating and reduce food insecurity
   - We have noticed that some of our regular attendees come from households where fresh fruit and vegetables are limited, often due to cost. Through this program, we will focus on growing easy-to-maintain, high-yield crops suited to Palmerston’s climate (e.g., cherry tomatoes, snake beans, Asian greens, okra, basil, and chillies). Harvested produce will be shared firstly with participating families and then donated to a local community pantry at a partner church in Gray.
   - We will also run two simple cooking demonstrations using the garden produce (e.g., stir-fries, salads, and herb-based sauces), facilitated by community members from Filipino, Sudanese, and Timorese backgrounds. This helps celebrate cultural diversity while encouraging healthier eating habits.

4. Strengthen community connections and inclusion
   - Our membership includes young people from at least 10 different cultural backgrounds (including Filipino, Sudanese, Timorese, Aboriginal, and Anglo-Australian). Working together in the garden gives them a shared project and reduces social isolation. We have already seen friendships form between youth who attend different schools and speak different first languages.
   - Parents and grandparents, many of whom have farming or gardening experience from their home countries, have expressed interest in coming along to share traditional knowledge. This intergenerational involvement helps older residents feel valued and included, and gives young people a stronger sense of identity and pride.

5. Improve local amenity and environmental awareness
   - By upgrading and maintaining a section of the Palmerston Community Garden, the program will make this public space more attractive and functional for all residents. We plan to build two new raised beds, install a simple drip irrigation system, and add interpretive signage (with both English and simple pictorial explanations) about composting, water-wise gardening, and how residents can start small gardens at home or on balconies.
   - Young participants will also undertake a small litter clean-up and basic weeding around the garden perimeter at the start of each session, helping to keep the area tidy and modelling positive stewardship of shared spaces.

6. Respond to demonstrated community need
   - In late 2024, we conducted an informal survey of 32 young people who attend our existing activities. Over 70% said they would like to learn more about growing food, and over 60% reported feeling stressed or anxious most weeks. Several specifically mentioned that working with plants made them feel calmer. The garden program is a low-cost, accessible way to respond to these needs.

In summary, the funding will allow us to purchase essential materials (soil, compost, mulch, seeds, seedlings, basic tools, and signage) and to offer a structured, inclusive program that supports youth wellbeing, improves local amenity, promotes sustainability, and strengthens social cohesion in Palmerston.
community_benefit__alignment.goals_and_strategies_in_the_city_of_palm:
Our proposal aligns with several key goals and strategies in the City of Palmerston Community Plan, particularly around community wellbeing, youth engagement, environmental sustainability, and inclusion.

1. A healthy, active, and engaged community
   - The weekly garden sessions will encourage young people to be physically active outdoors rather than indoors on screens. Activities such as digging, carrying watering cans, pushing wheelbarrows, and building garden beds all contribute to moderate physical activity in a way that is non-competitive and accessible to those who may not feel comfortable joining sports clubs.
   - We will include short, structured reflection circles at the end of each session where participants can talk about what they enjoyed, what they found challenging, and any ideas they have for the garden. This supports mental health by giving them a voice and a sense of ownership over the project.
   - This directly supports the Community Plan’s emphasis on encouraging healthy lifestyles and providing opportunities for residents to participate in community life.

2. Supporting young people to thrive
   - The program is specifically targeted at youth aged 12–25, with particular outreach to culturally and linguistically diverse (CALD) young people and those who are not engaged in mainstream extracurricular activities. We will promote the program through local schools (Palmerston College, Good Shepherd Lutheran College Palmerston), youth service providers, and social media.
   - By giving young people leadership roles (e.g., garden bed captains, compost coordinators, and session co-facilitators), we are building confidence, communication skills, and a sense of responsibility. Older participants will be encouraged to mentor younger ones, which aligns with the Community Plan’s goal of supporting youth leadership and positive role modelling.

3. An inclusive, multicultural, and connected city
   - Palmerston is highly multicultural, and our association reflects this diversity. The program will intentionally highlight different cultural approaches to growing and using food. For example, one of our members from South Sudan has volunteered to show how okra and leafy greens are grown and cooked in her home community, while a Timorese family will share how they use chillies and herbs.
   - We will encourage signage and garden bed names in multiple languages (e.g., English plus one or two community languages), which reinforces a sense of belonging and pride for CALD residents and supports the Community Plan’s objective of recognising and celebrating cultural diversity.
   - Open community days at the garden (we plan at least two during the 12-week program) will invite neighbours, families, and other residents to visit, taste produce, and learn about what the young people have achieved. This fosters social connections across age groups and backgrounds.

4. Environmental responsibility and sustainability
   - The program directly supports the Community Plan’s environmental goals by promoting sustainable urban living practices. We will teach participants about:
     • Composting food and garden waste to reduce landfill.
     • Water-wise gardening suited to the local climate (mulching, drip irrigation, and choosing drought-tolerant plants).
     • Growing food locally to reduce food miles and packaging waste.
   - We will document our processes and create simple, shareable information sheets (hard copy and digital) that residents can take home or access via our website and social media. This extends the environmental impact beyond the immediate group of participants.

5. Vibrant public spaces and a sense of place
   - The Palmerston Community Garden is a valued public asset, but parts of it are underutilised. By activating a specific area with regular youth-led activity, improved planting, and new signage, we will help transform it into a more vibrant and welcoming space in line with the Community Plan’s focus on attractive, functional public places.
   - We will work with the garden committee and Council staff (if appropriate) to ensure that the improvements we make are consistent with existing plans and that the garden remains accessible to the wider community.

6. Partnership and collaboration
   - The program is being developed in collaboration with the Palmerston Community Garden Committee and local service providers who work with young people. We have already had informal conversations with a youth worker from Anglicare NT who is keen to refer some of their clients who live in Palmerston. This collaborative approach reflects the Community Plan’s emphasis on partnerships between community groups, service providers, and Council to deliver better outcomes.

In essence, the project brings together multiple strands of the City of Palmerston Community Plan—youth engagement, health and wellbeing, inclusion, environmental sustainability, and activation of public spaces—into one practical, visible, and achievable initiative led by local young people.
